(**US 20A**) is an east–west [alternate route](/wiki/Alternate_route "Alternate route") of [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") that extends for 83.59 miles (134.53 km) across the [western portion](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It leaves US 20 in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"), a [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and rejoins it in [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York") about five miles (8.0 km) west of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York"), the county seat of [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"). The western terminus is situated just northeast of the intersection of US 20 and [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York") and west of [Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium"). At its east end, US 20A also meets [New York State Route 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") (NY 5) and [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64"). Most of the route is known as **Big Tree Road**; outside Highmark Stadium, the highway is known as the **Timothy J. Russert Highway** in memory of Buffalo native [Tim Russert](/wiki/Tim_Russert "Tim Russert").

(**NY 35**). US 20 was first signed in New York in 1927, occupying the piece of NY 35 between East Aurora and Geneseo and all of what is now US 20A west of East Aurora upon assignment. It was altered to also use the Geneseo–Lakeville section of former NY 35 in the early 1930s. At the time, the section of current US 20A between US 20 and [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York") was designated as [NY 254](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_254 "New York State Route 254"). US 20 was realigned onto its current alignment between Hamburg and Avon c. 1938, and US 20A was assigned to US 20's former routing west of Lakeville and all of NY 254 by the following year.

**U.S. Route 20A** (**US 20A**) is an east–west [alternate route](/wiki/Alternate_route "Alternate route") of [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") that extends for 83.59 miles (134.53 km) across the [western portion](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It leaves US 20 in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"), a [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and rejoins it in [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York") about five miles (8.0 km) west of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York"), the county seat of [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"). The western terminus is situated just northeast of the intersection of US 20 and [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York") and west of [Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium"). At its east end, US 20A also meets [New York State Route 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") (NY 5) and [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64"). Most of the route is known as **Big Tree Road**; outside Highmark Stadium, the highway is known as the **Timothy J. Russert Highway** in memory of Buffalo native [Tim Russert](/wiki/Tim_Russert "Tim Russert").

While the main line of US 20 takes a direct path between Hamburg, Buffalo, and East Bloomfield via [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York"), US 20A veers to the south to serve several villages and [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)"), including the villages of [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York") and [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw_(village),_New_York "Warsaw (village), New York"). The town of [Attica](/wiki/Attica,_New_York "Attica, New York"), famous for the [Attica Prison riot](/wiki/Attica_Prison_riot "Attica Prison riot") of 1971, lies between US 20 and US 20A. As the route heads east, US 20A connects to several north–south freeways, such as [NY 400](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_400 "New York State Route 400") outside of [East Aurora](/wiki/East_Aurora,_New_York "East Aurora, New York") and [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) between Geneseo and [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York"). Outside of the communities that dot the highway, US 20A is a two-lane, rural highway.

The highway carried several designations prior to becoming US 20A c. 1939. In the mid-1920s, the section of modern US 20A from East Aurora to Lakeville was part of **New York State Route 35** (**NY 35**). US 20 was first signed in New York in 1927, occupying the piece of NY 35 between East Aurora and Geneseo and all of what is now US 20A west of East Aurora upon assignment. It was altered to also use the Geneseo–Lakeville section of former NY 35 in the early 1930s. At the time, the section of current US 20A between US 20 and [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York") was designated as [NY 254](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_254 "New York State Route 254"). US 20 was realigned onto its current alignment between Hamburg and Avon c. 1938, and US 20A was assigned to US 20's former routing west of Lakeville and all of NY 254 by the following year.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

### Buffalo area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Buffalo area")]

US 20A separates from US 20 just northeast of an intersection locally known as "Seven Corners" in the town of [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg,_New_York "Hamburg, New York"). The route heads east through the [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") suburbs on Big Tree Road, entering the town of [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York") and passing south of the southern campus of [SUNY Erie](/wiki/SUNY_Erie "SUNY Erie").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At Abbott Road, US 20A becomes the "Timothy J. Russert Highway", named for [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") native [Tim Russert](/wiki/Tim_Russert "Tim Russert").[[4]](#cite_note-s3145-4) The route continues on, passing south of [Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium") before changing back to Big Tree Road at California Road.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[4]](#cite_note-s3145-4) Not far to the east, US 20A meets [US 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219_in_New_York "U.S. Route 219 in New York") ([Southern Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Expressway_(New_York) "Southern Expressway (New York)")) at an interchange. Past US 219, the route enters the village of [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(village),_New_York "Orchard Park (village), New York"), becoming Quaker Street and intersecting the [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") routes of [NY 240](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_240 "New York State Route 240") and [NY 277](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_277 "New York State Route 277") in the center of the community.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/US_20A_east_in_Orchard_Park_NY.jpg/250px-US_20A_east_in_Orchard_Park_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20A_east_in_Orchard_Park_NY.jpg)

Eastbound on US 20A in the village of Orchard Park

Outside of the village, US 20A becomes Quaker Road, passing through slightly less developed areas as it progresses eastward. Midway between Orchard Park village and [East Aurora](/wiki/East_Aurora,_New_York "East Aurora, New York"), US 20A intersects the southern terminus of [NY 187](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_187 "New York State Route 187") (Transit Road) at the Orchard Park–[Aurora](/wiki/Aurora,_Erie_County,_New_York "Aurora, Erie County, New York") town line. Transit Road, however, continues south for one mile (1.6 km) as [County Route 553](/wiki/County_Route_553_(Erie_County,_New_York) "County Route 553 (Erie County, New York)") (CR 553). Farther east, US 20A runs south of [Cazenovia Creek](/wiki/Cazenovia_Creek "Cazenovia Creek") for two miles (3.2 km) across open terrain prior to crossing the creek and entering the densely populated village of East Aurora.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The route enters East Aurora on Hamburg Street, running parallel to a branch of Cazenovia Creek for a half-mile (0.80 km) to Buffalo Street. Here, US 20A meets the overlapping routes of [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") and [NY 78](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_78 "New York State Route 78") at a [traffic circle](/wiki/Traffic_circle "Traffic circle") in the village's primary business district. The three routes come together, becoming Main Street and passing by the [Fillmore House](/wiki/Fillmore_House "Fillmore House") as it proceeds through the center of the village. At Olean Street, one mile (1.6 km) east of the circle in East Aurora's central business district, NY 16 turns right, continuing south toward [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). US 20A and NY 78 remain concurrent as they exit the village and meet [NY 400](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_400 "New York State Route 400") (Aurora Expressway) on the eastern edge.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### East Aurora to Warsaw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: East Aurora to Warsaw")]

East of NY 400, US 20A and NY 78 become Big Tree Road once more as the highway leaves the Buffalo suburbs and passes through the rural eastern portion of [Erie County](/wiki/Erie_County,_New_York "Erie County, New York"). Near the [Wales](/wiki/Wales,_New_York "Wales, New York") hamlet of Wales Center, NY 78 departs US 20A on Strykersville Road, ending the four-mile (6.4 km) overlap. In Wales Center itself, US 20A intersects Four Rod Road,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] once designated as [NY 358](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_358 "New York State Route 358")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and now part of [CR 356](/wiki/County_Route_356_(Erie_County,_New_York) "County Route 356 (Erie County, New York)"). Past the hamlet, US 20A deviates from its linear east–west alignment, swerving to the south on a newer routing of Big Tree Road before reuniting with its original routing one mile (1.6 km) to the east. Two miles (3.2 km) ahead, US 20A exits Erie County and enters the equally rural [Wyoming County](/wiki/Wyoming_County,_New_York "Wyoming County, New York") town of [Sheldon](/wiki/Sheldon,_New_York "Sheldon, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Warning_diagram_on_US_20A_NY.jpg/250px-Warning_diagram_on_US_20A_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Warning_diagram_on_US_20A_NY.jpg)

Sign on US 20A warning motorists of steep grades and sharp turns near Warsaw

In Sheldon, US 20A intersects [NY 77](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_77 "New York State Route 77") near Sheldon Center and [NY 98](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_98 "New York State Route 98") in [Varysburg](/wiki/Varysburg,_New_York "Varysburg, New York"), forming a concurrency with the latter into the adjacent town of [Orangeville](/wiki/Orangeville,_New_York "Orangeville, New York"). Upon crossing the town line, the two routes split, with NY 98 proceeding southwest to [Java](/wiki/Java,_New_York "Java, New York"). US 20A continues southeast from NY 98 on a linear alignment, intersecting the southern terminus of [NY 238](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_238 "New York State Route 238") at Halls Corner, a small hamlet located near the Orangeville–[Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw,_New_York "Warsaw, New York") town line. Shortly after passing NY 238, US 20A makes a turn to the east as it enters Warsaw. Roughly 1.5 miles (2.4 km) from the town line, US 20A descends into the Oatka Valley, a large ravine containing [Oatka Creek](/wiki/Oatka_Creek "Oatka Creek") and the entirety of the [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw_(village),_New_York "Warsaw (village), New York"). Now named Buffalo Street, US 20A heads east–west across the village, intersecting [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19"), one of two state routes to directly connect [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") with the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border"), at the center of Warsaw.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Past the NY 19 junction, US 20A continues east along Buffalo Street to the eastern edge of the village and the valley, where the route turns a full 90 degrees to the north to climb the side of the Oatka Valley.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The route ascends approximately 300 feet (91 m),[[6]](#cite_note-warsaw_topo-6) passing under the [Rochester and Southern Railroad](/wiki/Rochester_and_Southern_Railroad "Rochester and Southern Railroad") (RSR)—which runs along the foot of the gorge—before exiting the valley and returning to an east–west alignment as it heads toward [Perry](/wiki/Perry_(town),_New_York "Perry (town), New York") and points east.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] All oversize trucks are prohibited from using the section of US 20A between NY 19 and Minor Road at the top of the valley due to the steep grade of the hill and the sharp curve at the bottom.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Warsaw to Geneseo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Warsaw to Geneseo")]

Outside of the Oatka Valley, US 20A heads east through the remainder of the town of Warsaw prior to entering the town of Perry. The route serves [Perry–Warsaw Airport](/wiki/Perry%E2%80%93Warsaw_Airport "Perry–Warsaw Airport") on its way to Perry Center, a small hamlet situated directly north of the village of [Perry](/wiki/Perry_(village),_New_York "Perry (village), New York"), where US 20A intersects [NY 246](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_246 "New York State Route 246"). The portion of US 20A in Perry is otherwise uneventful, and the route quietly passes into the [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") town of [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_(town),_New_York "Leicester (town), New York") 2.5 miles (4.0 km) later. After another one mile (1.6 km) of rural surroundings, US 20A meets [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") at a junction approximately 1.5 miles (2.4 km) southwest of the village of [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_(village),_New_York "Leicester (village), New York"). The two routes converge, following Leicester Road northeast to the village, where the highway's name shifts to Main Street upon entering the village limits.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/US_20A-NY_39_in_Geneseo.jpg/250px-US_20A-NY_39_in_Geneseo.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20A-NY_39_in_Geneseo.jpg)

US 20A and NY 39 in the town of Geneseo

East of a [grade crossing](/wiki/Grade_crossing "Grade crossing") with the [Genesee and Wyoming Railroad](/wiki/Genesee_and_Wyoming_Railroad "Genesee and Wyoming Railroad") subdivision of the RSR, US 20A and NY 39 connect to [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") at York Road. NY 36 briefly overlaps with US 20A and NY 39 for one block before heading to the south via Mount Morris Road at the village center. US 20A and NY 39 continue on, leaving Leicester on as Cuylerville Road, named for [the community](/wiki/Cuylerville,_New_York "Cuylerville, New York") located directly northeast of Leicester. The routes pass in and out of Cuylerville and head eastward into a wide valley, crossing Beards Creek and the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River"). At the eastern bank of the latter, the highway crosses into the town of [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo,_New_York "Geneseo, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On the other side of the Genesee River, US 20A and NY 39 rise in elevation once more as they head to the northeast, climbing the eastern edge of the gully to meet [NY 63](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_63 "New York State Route 63") at the valley's eastern edge. After a brief concurrency with US 20A and NY 39, NY 63 splits off to the west, running alongside the western edge of the nearby [State University of New York at Geneseo](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_at_Geneseo "State University of New York at Geneseo") campus. In contrast, US 20A and NY 39 form a portion of the eastern edge of the campus between NY 63 and [Mary Jemison](/wiki/Mary_Jemison "Mary Jemison") Drive in the village of [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York"). At the latter intersection, US 20A and NY 39 turn east onto South Street. After just one block, the US 20A/NY 39 overlap comes to an end as NY 39 follows Main Street north out of Geneseo toward [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

US 20A continues east through Geneseo before taking on a more northeasterly alignment outside of the village. At this point, the highway becomes Lakeville Road for the [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake") community to the northeast. The route proceeds across open terrain to the western outskirts of [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York"), where US 20A connects to [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") at exit 8. Past the expressway, US 20A continues on a northeast alignment—albeit more eastern than northern—as it intersects [NY 256](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_256 "New York State Route 256") and enters both the town of [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia,_New_York "Livonia, New York") and the hamlet of Lakeville.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Finger Lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Finger Lakes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/US_20A_and_NY_15_1_edit.jpg/250px-US_20A_and_NY_15_1_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20A_and_NY_15_1_edit.jpg)

US 20A east and NY 15 south in Lakeville

As Big Tree Road once more, US 20A acts as the major east–west thoroughfare for Lakeville, connecting the hamlet to I-390 in the west to the village of [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York") to the east. In the center of Lakeville, US 20A intersects the north–south [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"). The two routes converge on Big Tree Road, following the roadway around the northern tip of Conesus Lake and southeastward through the rural landscape between Lakeville and Livonia. In Livonia, Big Tree Road continues to the southeast as Big Tree Street; however, US 20A and NY 15 leave Big Tree Road to follow Main Street instead. NY 15 remains on US 20A for one more block to the center of Livonia, at which point it continues southward on Commercial Street toward [Conesus](/wiki/Conesus,_New_York "Conesus, New York") while US 20A heads east on Main Street and out of the village as Richmond Mills Road.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For the next 2.5 miles (4.0 km), US 20A loosely parallels Big Tree Road, located about one mile (1.6 km) to the south. At a rural junction known as Bosley Corner, US 20A merges with the north–south [NY 15A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15A "New York State Route 15A") and follows NY 15A south toward the hamlet of [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock,_New_York "Hemlock, New York"). While concurrent with NY 15A, US 20A crosses Big Tree Road at the midpoint of the concurrency. The two routes proceed through Hemlock before splitting south of the community. Here, US 20A breaks from NY 15A and continues east on Honeoye Road into the [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York") town of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_New_York "Richmond, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

About 2.5 miles (4.0 km) into the sparsely developed town, US 20A reconnects to Big Tree Road at Curtis Corner. The route, however, becomes Main Street for the hamlet of [Honeoye](/wiki/Honeoye,_New_York "Honeoye, New York") a short distance to the east. US 20A runs east–west through the small yet highly developed community, crossing [Honeoye Creek](/wiki/Honeoye_Creek "Honeoye Creek") and passing north of [Honeoye Lake](/wiki/Honeoye_Lake "Honeoye Lake"). It leaves the hamlet soon after, turning to the northeast and traversing a series of small hills as it passes into the town of [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York") as Honeoye Road once again. Roughly four miles (6.4 km) from the town line, US 20A intersects [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64") in the [Bristol Valley](/wiki/Bristol_Valley "Bristol Valley"). US 20A turns north onto NY 64, following the state route out of the valley and into the town of [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York"), where US 20A ends four miles (6.4 km) later at an intersection with US 20 and NY 5. The section of US 20A that overlaps NY 64 was signed north–south, matching NY 64's direction, however, the US 20A north–south tabs were replaced by east–west tabs between 2010 and 2012, but still the north–south tabs are now for NY 64 shields only. NY 64 continues west from the junction on US 20 and NY 5.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins and designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Origins and designation")]

In 1908, the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") established a statewide [legislative route](/wiki/Legislative_route "Legislative route") system that initially consisted of 37 [unsigned](/wiki/Unsigned_highway "Unsigned highway") routes. The portion of what is now US 20A between Four Rod Road in [Wales](/wiki/Wales,_New_York "Wales, New York") and [NY 98](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_98 "New York State Route 98") in [Sheldon](/wiki/Sheldon,_New_York "Sheldon, New York") was included in Route 19, a highway that extended from [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") to [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York"). Two smaller segments of modern US 20A—the sections [concurrent](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") to [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") in [East Aurora](/wiki/East_Aurora,_New_York "East Aurora, New York") and [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64") in [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York")—became part of Route 17 and Route 14, respectively.[[8]](#cite_note-1909book-8)[[9]](#cite_note-1920book-9) When the first set of posted routes in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") were assigned in 1924, the entirety of legislative Route 17 was designated as part of [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") while the portion of modern US 20A between [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York") and [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York") became part of [NY 4](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_4_(1924%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 4 (1924–1927)"). By 1926, the section of what is now US 20A between NY 16 in East Aurora and NY 4 in Lakeville was designated as part of NY 35, which initially continued northwest to Buffalo by way of an overlap with NY 16 and followed NY 4 north from Lakeville to [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in [East Avon](/wiki/East_Avon,_New_York "East Avon, New York").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/US_20A_at_NY_15A_S_of_Hemlock.jpg/250px-US_20A_at_NY_15A_S_of_Hemlock.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20A_at_NY_15A_S_of_Hemlock.jpg)

Approaching NY 15A on US 20A westbound near Hemlock. Note the errant NY 20A shield.

NY 35 was largely replaced by [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") when [U.S. Highways](/wiki/U.S._Highway "U.S. Highway") were first signed in New York in 1927. At the time, US 20 entered the Buffalo area on modern NY 5 and followed Big Tree Road and current US 20A east to East Aurora, where it overlapped NY 16 to reach the former routing of NY 35. From there, it continued east over former NY 35 to [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York") before proceeding to [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York") on modern [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39"). Meanwhile, NY 4 was renumbered to [NY 2](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_2_(1927%E2%80%931939) "New York State Route 2 (1927–1939)") to eliminate numerical duplication with the new [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York") in the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District_(New_York) "Capital District (New York)").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The portion of former NY 35 between Geneseo and Lakeville went unnumbered[[13]](#cite_note-13) until the [1930 state highway renumbering](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)") when it became the westernmost portion of NY 254, an east–west highway that initially extended eastward along modern US 20A[[14]](#cite_note-1930map-14) and [CR 32](/wiki/County_Route_32_(Ontario_County,_New_York) "County Route 32 (Ontario County, New York)") to a junction with [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") west of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua,_New_York "Canandaigua, New York").[[15]](#cite_note-15) NY 254 was truncated westward to NY 64 in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York") by the following year.[[16]](#cite_note-1931map-16)

US 20 was realigned between Geneseo and East Avon c. 1931 to follow NY 254 east to Lakeville, where it turned north to follow NY 2 to East Avon. NY 254 was truncated to the east end of its former overlap with NY 2 in [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(village),_New_York "Livonia (village), New York") as a result while US 20's former routing between Geneseo and Avon became [NY 20D](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20D "New York State Route 20D").[[14]](#cite_note-1930map-14)[[16]](#cite_note-1931map-16) US 20 was altered again c. 1938 to follow its modern alignment between [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg_(town),_New_York "Hamburg (town), New York") and East Avon. Its former routing between Hamburg and Geneseo became part of the new NY 20A, which also continued north to Avon over NY 20D. The remainder of US 20's old routing between Geneseo and Lakeville became part of a reextended NY 254.[[2]](#cite_note-1938map-2)[[17]](#cite_note-17) US 20A was established by the following year, replacing NY 20A west of Geneseo and NY 254 from Geneseo to Bristol. The new highway also followed NY 64 northward to [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York") in order to rejoin US 20 in the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of South Bloomfield. NY 254 was reassigned to the Geneseo–Avon portion of NY 20A;[[3]](#cite_note-1939map-3) however, it was supplanted by an extended NY 39 c. 1940.[[3]](#cite_note-1939map-3)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Other developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_20A_(New_York)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Timothy_J_Russert_Highway_sign.jpg/250px-Timothy_J_Russert_Highway_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Timothy_J_Russert_Highway_sign.jpg)

Timothy J. Russert Highway sign at US 20A and Abbott Road

On the morning of March 12, 1994, a large section of the roof in the southern end of the [Retsof Salt Mine](/wiki/Retsof_Salt_Mine "Retsof Salt Mine") collapsed. The collapsed portion of the mine was located near the hamlet of [Cuylerville](/wiki/Cuylerville,_New_York "Cuylerville, New York"), located on US 20A just south of the mine.[[19]](#cite_note-collapse-nyt-19) One day before the collapse, warning signs were erected at the bridge carrying US 20A over nearby Beards Creek due to the presence of bumps in the pavement along the bridge approaches. Over the next few weeks, two large sinkholes opened on the surface above the mine, one of them located just south of the US 20A bridge over Beards Creek. This sinkhole led to the collapse of a small area of US 20A east of the creek and the closure and, ultimately, collapse of the bridge over the creek.[[20]](#cite_note-collapse-usgs-20) As a result, a detour was installed between [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_(village),_New_York "Leicester (village), New York") and Geneseo.[[21]](#cite_note-clarionakzo103-21) No construction was done on the area for a number of years while a court battle was fought over whether Retsof should reimburse the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") to fix the road.[[22]](#cite_note-clarionakzo104-22) The road and new bridge were fixed and reopened in the late 1990s.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On June 18, 2008, a bill seeking to rename the part of US 20A from Abbott Road to California Road as the "Timothy J. Russert Highway" was introduced in the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/U.S._Senate "U.S. Senate"). The bill, sponsored by Senators [Chuck Schumer](/wiki/Chuck_Schumer "Chuck Schumer") and [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") along with Representative [Brian Higgins](/wiki/Brian_Higgins "Brian Higgins"), was passed by both the [House](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and the Senate and was signed into law by President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") on July 23, 2008. The designation is in memory of Buffalo native [Tim Russert](/wiki/Tim_Russert "Tim Russert"), the long-time host of NBC's *[Meet the Press](/wiki/Meet_the_Press "Meet the Press")* who died on June 13, 2008.[[4]](#cite_note-s3145-4)
